questions.question: Briefly explain the concept of warp divergence in GPU computing and describe its negative effect on performance.
keyPoints: A warp is a group of threads (e.g., 32 threads) executed in lockstep on a GPU SM.
Warp divergence occurs when threads within a single warp take different execution paths due to conditional statements (e.g., if/else).
It forces the SM to serialize the execution of the different paths, disabling threads not on the current path, which leads to significant underutilization of the processor and a loss of performance.

questions.question: Describe the key architectural features of a GPU that make it suitable for parallel computing, and explain the concept of latency hiding through massive multithreading.
keyPoints: GPUs have a massively parallel architecture composed of many Streaming Multiprocessors (SMs), each containing numerous cores (CUDA Cores/Streaming Processors).
They feature a large register file and high bandwidth memory hierarchies (shared memory, caches, DRAM) to support data access for thousands of concurrent threads.

Latency hiding is achieved by having a huge number of threads available for execution. When one group of threads (a warp) stalls waiting for data from memory, the SM immediately switches to executing instructions from another available warp, keeping the cores busy and effectively hiding the memory access latency.

questions.question: Compare and contrast the performance characteristics and optimal use cases for shared memory and global memory in a CUDA-enabled GPU. Your answer should detail the latency, bandwidth, scope, and a common programming strategy (like tiling) used to optimize performance.
keyPoints: Global memory is the high-capacity DRAM (e.g., GDDR6/HBM) on the GPU card. It has high bandwidth but also high latency (hundreds of cycles) and is accessible to all threads in a grid.

Shared memory is a low-latency, high-bandwidth software-managed cache on each SM. Its scope is limited to the threads within a thread block, making it much faster than global memory but also much smaller in capacity.

A common strategy to optimize performance is 'tiling': Data is loaded from high-latency global memory into shared memory in a collaborative fashion by all threads in a block. Subsequent computations are then performed on this locally cached data, significantly reducing the number of costly global memory accesses and improving overall kernel performance, especially for algorithms with data reuse like matrix multiplication.
